search_text: 5BE3A5CC__An Act relating to wildlife regulation.pdf other 2026 regulation  This is an Act requesting amendments to hb 168 before it proceeds further through legislation.  It  relates to wildlife regulation; amending Title 23A, Chapter 4, Part 12; clarifying regulatory  authority; creating the Utah Outfitter and Guide Board; establishing professional qualification  standards; providing for adjudicative review; requiring federal permit documentation for  outfitters operating on federal land; requiring public registry publication; and making technical  conforming amendments.  Section 1. Amend Title 23A, Chapter 4, Part  12.  23A-4-1202. Definitions.  For purposes of this part:  (1) “Division” means the Division of Wildlife Resources acting in its regulatory capacity. (2) “Board” means the Utah Outfitter and Guide Board created in Section 23A-4-1204.5 (3) “Compensation” means anything of value, direct or indirect, monetary or nonmonetary, received or expected in exchange for outfitting or guiding services. Compensation does not include incidental or de minimis as

Both the Lives of the Outﬁtters and Guides  that work in this industry and the future of this industry and you are also going to be making  decisions that will directly impact Guide Service Operations on the ground and how  effective, how safe and how legal or illegal they are enabled to be.  Utah Code 23 has been messed up in it’s language for a long time.  SB 149 was taken from  Code 23 and then from SB 149 the DWR attempted to draft  R657-72.  The language from  the top down with each piece of legislation in this case was ﬂawed from the start, and as it  ﬂowed downhill it did so like an Avalanche until it basically piled up at the bottom in an  enormous mess. search_text: 672FAE79__Predictive Engine for Hunts.pdf other 2026 harvest_report  Predictive Engine for Hunts Executive summary Building a predictive hunting engine is feasible, but it will only be as credible as its data provenance, bias controls, and  validation design. The fastest path to a trustworthy prototype is a  hybrid system: (a) a rigorous geospatial constraints layer (public access, legal seasons/units, closures), (b) a habitat–weather opportunity model that outputs calibrated probabilities on a spatial grid, and (c) a  user feedback loop that steadily improves labels and personalization while protecting privacy and avoiding wildlife disturbance.
